does: VeriGate extends Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with verifier-gated step-level supervision to address the problem of collapsed group-relative advantages when all trajectories receive identical outcome rewards. The method employs three key mechanisms: a gating strategy that preferentially uses verifier rewards when they induce meaningful preference among trajectories and falls back to process supervision (PRM scores) only when verifier rewards are degenerate; conversion of PRM step scores into future-cumulated rewards that provide continuation-aware credit assignment across trajectory steps; and transformation of these rewards into group-normalized token-level advantages to maintain informative gradient signals during training. This design maintains the efficiency of outcome-level verification while adding fine-grained step supervision where needed, improving exploration and robustness in reasoning model training.
connects: This work builds on the GRPO framework, which applies group relative policy optimization to reasoning models trained with verifier-based rewards. VeriGate responds to a fundamental limitation of outcome-only reward supervision: the sparse reward problem where identical verifier scores across sampled outputs eliminate the relative advantages needed for learning. The paper extends prior work on process reward models (PRMs) in reasoning tasks and combines outcome and process supervision in a hybrid approach. It relates to broader research on credit assignment in reinforcement learning and the integration of verifier feedback with step-level supervision, addressing challenges similar to those tackled in previous work on reasoning model training like outcomes supervision for math and code generation tasks.
enables: VeriGate enables more efficient and robust training of reasoning models by resolving the sparse reward problem in group-relative optimization, potentially accelerating convergence and improving solution quality. The verifier-gating mechanism suggests future work on dynamic supervision strategies that adaptively select between different reward signals based on their informativeness. The continuation-aware credit assignment opens directions for better understanding which intermediate reasoning steps contribute most to solution quality. More broadly, this work enables exploration of hybrid outcome-process supervision strategies in other domains requiring step-wise reasoning. It points toward more sophisticated verification approaches that combine fast outcome verification with selective step-level guidance, potentially improving data efficiency and generalization in reasoning model training across mathematics, code generation, and other complex reasoning tasks.
